questions.question: A developer says, 'Continuous Deployment means every change must be manually approved before going to production.' Which part of this statement is incorrect, and why?
options: The statement is fully correct — manual approval is always required in CD.
Continuous Deployment automatically ships every passing change all the way to production; Continuous Delivery is the variant that keeps a manual approval gate before production.
Continuous Deployment means deploying only once a week with manual checks.
Both Continuous Delivery and Continuous Deployment require a manual approval step before production.
questions.explanation: Option B is correct. Continuous Delivery keeps every change in a deployable state but may retain a final manual approval gate. Continuous Deployment removes that gate entirely and ships every change that passes automated checks directly to production. The other options confuse the two terms or invent a weekly deployment cadence that is not part of either definition.

questions.question: Which of the following best explains why ordering pipeline stages as Source → Build → Test → Deploy protects a production environment?
options: It ensures that all developers merge their code at the same time, avoiding conflicts.
Each stage acts as a gate: a failure stops the pipeline before the artifact progresses, so untested or broken code can never reach the deploy stage.
Running stages in sequence is faster than running them in parallel.
The Deploy stage cannot start until the Source stage has been running for at least five minutes.
questions.explanation: Option B captures the core principle of the four-stage pipeline described in the lecture. Because each stage only passes control to the next if it succeeds, a build failure prevents testing, and a test failure prevents image creation and deployment. This 'fail fast' pattern is what keeps bad code from reaching users. Options A, C, and D describe unrelated or fabricated mechanics.

questions.question: In the course pipeline, which component receives the webhook notification from GitLab and then executes the build, test, and deploy stages?
options: AWS EC2
AWS ECR
Jenkins
Docker Compose
questions.explanation: Jenkins is the automation server in this pipeline. When a developer pushes code, GitLab sends an HTTP POST (the webhook) to Jenkins, which then checks out the code, runs tests, builds a Docker image, pushes it to ECR, and deploys to EC2. EC2 is the deployment target, ECR is the image registry, and Docker Compose is the container orchestration tool on EC2 — none of them react directly to the GitLab push event.

questions.question: A team is still deploying manually on a weekly basis, and they experience a failed deployment at 9 p.m. on a Friday. Which characteristic of manual deployment does this scenario illustrate?
options: Manual deployment is fast but lacks traceability.
Manual deployment is reproducible but depends on a senior engineer being available.
Manual deployment is slow, stressful, and depends on a human being available — making off-hours failures hard to recover from.
Manual deployment is safe because a senior engineer reviews every step.
questions.explanation: The lecture explicitly describes this scenario: if something breaks at 9 p.m. and nobody is watching, customers are stuck until morning. This illustrates that manual deployment requires a human to be available, awake, and careful — and when they are not, recovery is delayed. Option A is wrong because manual deployment is not fast. Option B is wrong because reproducibility is exactly what manual deployment lacks. Option D romanticises manual review as safety, which the lecture contradicts.

questions.question: After completing this course, what happens between the moment a developer runs 'git push' and the moment the new version is live on EC2?
options: The developer must SSH into EC2 and manually run docker compose up.
A GitLab CI job builds and deploys the image without involving Jenkins.
Jenkins checks out the code, builds and tests it, builds a Docker image, pushes it to ECR, SSHes into EC2, swaps the container, and confirms with a health check — all automatically.
The new image is emailed to an operations team who deploy it during business hours.
questions.explanation: Option C accurately describes the automated end-to-end flow presented in the lecture's pipeline overview: GitLab webhook → Jenkins → checkout → build/test → Docker image → ECR push → EC2 SSH → docker compose → health check / rollback. Option A describes the manual process the pipeline replaces. Option B is wrong because Jenkins, not a GitLab CI job, orchestrates the pipeline. Option D is fictional.
